items.title: Protest rallies take place across US against Trump policies | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Rallies are taking place across the United States to protest the policies of President Donald Trump, which critics call a threat to democracy. Thousands of protesters joined a rally here in Washington D.C., with hundreds of other protests planned for across the country. Among other things, demonstrators are angry about Trump's immigration crackdown, economic uncertainty from sweeping tariffs, and Trump slashing the federal workforce. Millions joined similar protests earlier this month.

Meantime, the US Supreme Court has responded to an emergency appeal and ordered the Trump administration to halt any further deportations of Venezuelan migrants. The White House had invoked a law from 1798 to round up and remove those they identified as gang members. Trump officials have already defied earlier court orders to reign in their campaign to deport migrants. 

Chapters:
0:00 US Supreme Court responds to appeal
2:40 Thousands rally against Trump policies
3:12 Stefan Simons, DW Reporter
7:14 Frank Sesno, Journalist

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Iran describes nuclear talks with US as being constructive | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Iran's Foreign Minister has described talks with a US delegation in Rome over Tehran's nuclear programme “constructive” and “moving forward.”

Abbas Araghchi told an Iranian broadcaster that he hoped Tehran will be in a better position to, quote “judge the possibility of reaching an agreement”. At their second indirect meeting in a week, Araghchi negotiated for almost four hours in Rome with Trump's Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff. An Omani official shuttled messages between them. A third round of negotiations is expected to take place next week.

Chapters:
0:00 The latest on US-Iran nuclear talks
0:37 Alex Vatanka, Middle East Institute

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Breaking: Putin announced unilateral Easter truce for Ukraine | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Russian President Vladimir Putin has announced a unilateral Easter truce for the war in Ukraine. In a televised address, Putin said the pause in fighting would begin this evening, 19.04, and last through to Sunday. He said Russian troops should be "ready to repel violations and provocations by Ukraine." Meanwhile, Kyiv said drones have been spotted above the city. And Ukraine's President Zelenskyy called the truce "another attempt by Putin to play with human lives." 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Iran, US to meet in Rome for talks on nuclear deal | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Diplomats from Iran and the United States are due to meet in Rome on Saturday for negotiations over Tehran's rapidly advancing nuclear program. 
Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi arrived in Rome earlier today. It's the second round of talks, following a meeting in Oman last weekend. Iran told the US it was ready to accept some limits on uranium enrichment, but needed strong guarantees that any deal would hold. 
Araghchi was in Russia on Friday, where he expressed skepticism over the intentions of the US, which scrapped a previous nuclear agreement signed during President Donald Trump's first term. 

00:00 Iran, US to meet for nuclear talks
01:07 DW speaks with Simon Mabon, expert in international relations and terrorism

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: US Supreme Court blocks Trump's plan to send Venezuelans to El Salvador | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The US Supreme Court has ordered the Trump administration not to deport a group of Venezuelan migrants accused of being gang members. President Trump had invoked the Alien Enemies Act from 1798 to round up the migrants -- that's an obscure law last used during World War Two. 
The court order to pause the deportations came after an emergency appeal by lawyers for the Venezuelans. They are currently being held in detention in Texas. The temporary halt is the latest ruling by the Supreme Court against Trump's deportation plans. Officials are already defying an earlier court decision to return a wrongfully expelled man from El Salvador.
US Senator Chris van Hollen has traveled to El Salvador to meet with that man, Kilmar Abrego Garcia. He said the US judicial system itself was under threat.

00:00 US Supreme Court blocks removal of Venezuelans to El Salvador
03:15 DW speaks with US analyst William Glucroft, Deputy Editor for Parliament Magazine in Brussels

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Will the withdrawal of most U.S. troops leave a vacuum that Turkey will try to fill? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The U.S. is withdrawing hundreds of troops and closing some of its bases in northeastern Syria. The withdrawal will leave about 1,000 troops - about half the number there now - to work with Kurdish allies to prevent a resurgence of the so-called Islamic State militant group. US President Donald Trump attempted to withdraw all US forces from Syria during his first term, but faced opposition from the Pentagon, which saw it as abandoning allies.

Chapters:
00:00 US withdrawing hundreds of troops from Syria
00:32 Caroline Rose, Director at the New Lines Institute

items.title: Could recycling beat China's rare earth trump card? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>China has suspended the export of certain critical minerals to the United States, in response to Donald Trump's trade war. Among them are so-called rare earths. These are heavy metals found in the Earth's crust, and they are vital components in a range of modern gadgets. 

Chapters:
00:00 China tightens export controls on rare earth minerals
01:30 Elisabeth Braw, Atlantic Council

items.title: Why is the US attacking not just the Houthis' military but their economic infrastructure? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Yemen's Houthi rebels say dozens of people have been killed in a US strike on a fuel port on the Red Sea. It's the deadliest known attack in Washington's 15-month campaign against the group, which is backed by Iran. The US says the strike was intended to cut off supplies and funds to the rebels, who have been attacking ships and firing missiles at Israel.

Chapters:
00:00 US strikes destroy Yemen fuel port used by houthi rebels
02:20 Veena Ali-Khan, Middle East Analyst

items.title: International tourists decide against visiting Trump's America | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>International tourism to the US taken a hit since the start of the Trump administration. Global trade tensions are playing a part, but many travellers are also put off by strict border controls and harrowing stories of random arrests. Germans are among those caught up in Trump's migration crackdown.

The number of visitors from Canada, formerly the biggest source of international tourists to the US, has declined steeply, with many joining a boycott against the US, following President Trump's threats to annex Canada.

DW spoke to one Canadian who says she won't be visiting the US again until Trump is out of office.

#usa #tourism #trump

00:00 US visitor numbers decline steeply
02:28 Canadian explains why she won't be visiting the US

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Trial against anti-govt protesters begins in Turkey | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Friends and family members gathered to show support for 189 people that were arrested in March during massive anti-government protests in Istanbul. The protesters, who gathered in support of jailed Istanbul Mayor Ekrem Imamoglu, were charged with failing to disperse when ordered to, carrying weapons, inciting crime and covering their faces.

items.title: Will trials over Imamoglu protests bring more demonstrators to the streets? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The trial has begun in Turkey of nearly 200 people arrested in recent anti-government protests. The group is accused of attending banned demonstrations in support of jailed opposition mayor Ekrem Imamoglu and disobeying police orders to disperse. Most of the defendants are students, but a number of journalists have also been charged. They face up to four years behind bars. 

DW's correspondent Dorian Jones joined us from Istanbul with the latest.


00:00 Turkey protest mass trials begin
02:33 DW's Dorian Jones explains how opposition has reacted to trials

items.title: Easter potatoes? Americans get creative with decorations thanks to soaring egg prices | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Bird flu, inflation and cacao swollen shoot virus are all changing how people are celebrating Easter this year. People are dyeing potatoes instead of eggs because egg prices in the US skyrocketed because of bird flu outbreaks. And much higher cocoa bean costs are driving chocolate prices up and production down


#easter #eggs #holidays
